heroOverview: Androphobia is an other specified specific phobia (ICD-10: F40.290) characterised by intense, persistent, and functionally impairing fear of men or male figures. Unlike normal social discomfort, androphobia produces an immediate anxiety or panic response triggered by the presence, proximity, or anticipated contact with men — and drives significant avoidance of situations where men are likely to be encountered. The condition is frequently, though not exclusively, associated with a history of interpersonal trauma such as sexual assault, physical abuse, or domestic violence; it can also arise through conditioning, observational learning, or comorbid anxiety disorders without a single identifiable traumatic event. Prevalence data specific to androphobia are limited, but it falls within the broader category of other specified phobias, which together affect an estimated 5-7% of the global adult population. Trauma-informed cognitive-behavioural therapy with carefully graded exposure is the recommended first-line treatment.

keyStats.prevalence: Precise prevalence data are limited; androphobia falls within other specified specific phobias (F40.290), which affect an estimated 5-7% of adults globally (Wardenaar et al., 2017)
keyStats.demographics: Reported more frequently in women and survivors of male-perpetrated trauma; can affect any gender
keyStats.avgAge: Onset commonly follows a traumatic event in adolescence or adulthood; no firm median age across unselected samples
keyStats.globalCases: Exact figures unavailable; estimated in the millions as part of the broader specific phobia category

definition: Androphobia (ICD-10: F40.290) is a specific phobia classified under other specified phobia codes, defined by a marked and excessive fear or anxiety reliably cued by the presence, proximity, or anticipated encounter with men or male individuals. The term combines the Greek aner/andros (man) and phobos (fear). The fear response is disproportionate to actual risk, persists for at least six months, and produces significant distress or functional impairment in social, occupational, or personal domains. Triggering contexts typically include any situation involving being alone with one or more men, encountering unfamiliar men in public, physical proximity, eye contact, or hearing a man's voice. The phobia is clinically and aetiologically distinct from general social anxiety (which is not gender-specific) and from misandry (a cultural or philosophical stance rather than an anxiety disorder). Androphobia sits within the psychiatry and clinical psychology specialties and is most rigorously managed by therapists trained in both trauma-informed care and CBT-based exposure, given the high prevalence of prior traumatic experiences in this population. Careful clinical distinction must be made between androphobia and the hypervigilance and avoidance of PTSD — the two can co-occur, and the presence of PTSD does not exclude a comorbid specific phobia diagnosis when avoidance has become stimulus-specific and functionally disabling beyond what PTSD alone explains.

typesClassification.description: Develops following direct experience of male-perpetrated interpersonal trauma — sexual assault, physical or emotional abuse, or domestic violence — through direct fear conditioning; PTSD frequently co-occurs and may require parallel treatment.

Develops through observational learning, information transmission, or an insidious onset without a single identifiable traumatic event; anxiety is reliably triggered by men but does not meet PTSD criteria.

Characterised by full panic attacks — chest tightness, breathlessness, dizziness, depersonalisation — upon encountering men; requires assessment for comorbid panic disorder and may need medication before exposure is possible.

Functional impairment is primarily confined to the workplace or professional contexts; the person manages family and domestic life but cannot work effectively in settings with male colleagues, supervisors, or clients.

primarySymptoms: Immediate and intense fear or panic upon seeing, approaching, or being approached by a man — the core symptom that must produce distress or impairment for a clinical diagnosis.
Rapid heart rate, chest tightness, and breathlessness as the autonomic fight-or-flight response activates within seconds of perceived proximity to a man.
Sweating, trembling, and cold extremities accompanying the adrenaline surge triggered by the phobic stimulus.
Urge to flee, hide, or escape immediately when a man approaches or enters the same space.
Freezing response — inability to move or speak — when unexpectedly confronted by a man in close proximity.
Derealization or depersonalization — a sensation of unreality or detachment — during intense androphobic episodes, particularly those linked to trauma re-activation.
Persistent, distressing anticipatory anxiety before any situation likely to involve men — public transport, medical appointments, workplaces, or social events.
Extensive avoidance behaviours — refusing to travel alone, avoiding workplaces with male staff, limiting shopping to women-only hours, or seeking only female medical practitioners.
Intrusive thoughts or mental images relating to past traumatic experiences when in proximity to men, triggering fear beyond what the current situation warrants.
Physical tension, startle responses, and hypervigilance in public spaces where men are present — scanning exits, maintaining maximum distance from male strangers.
earlyWarningSigns: Mild unease or discomfort when a man unexpectedly enters the same room or space, before full panic develops.
Progressive narrowing of acceptable environments — gradually eliminating situations where men are likely to be present.
Unusual hypervigilance in public, characterised by constant awareness of male presence, before frank phobic avoidance becomes obvious.
Reluctance to consult male doctors, dentists, or service providers that increasingly limits access to care.
emergencySigns: Full panic attack with chest pain, severe breathlessness, and a belief of imminent harm — seek emergency assessment if this is a first episode and cardiac cause has not been excluded.
Severe androphobia that prevents access to emergency medical care — for example, refusal to accept treatment from male first responders or hospital staff during a medical emergency.
Acute suicidal ideation triggered by perceived inescapable exposure to men, particularly in the context of comorbid PTSD or depression.
Complete social and occupational isolation resulting from inability to encounter any male-presenting individuals, producing a medical emergency through self-neglect.

causes.cause: Direct traumatic conditioning from male-perpetrated interpersonal violence
causes.description: Sexual assault, physical abuse, domestic violence, or stalking by a male perpetrator can establish a conditioned fear response in which men as a stimulus class reliably trigger the threat response — the most clinically common aetiology for androphobia.

causes.cause: Childhood exposure to domestic violence or neglect
causes.description: Witnessing or experiencing male-perpetrated violence or severe neglect in a household during childhood can establish deep conditioning associations between men and danger through direct and observational learning during formative developmental windows.

Repeated exposure to accounts, imagery, or media depicting men as threatening — whether through cultural messaging, community trauma, or repeated exposure to others' victimisation narratives — can install fear associations without direct personal trauma.

causes.cause: Generalisation from a specific threatening male individual
causes.description: A fear initially conditioned to a specific perpetrator or threatening individual can generalise across all men through stimulus generalisation, particularly when the original conditioning was severe and repeated.

causes.cause: Hyperactive threat-appraisal system and anxiety sensitivity
causes.description: Individuals with high trait anxiety and anxiety sensitivity may develop androphobia after minimal traumatic input, as their threat-appraisal circuits assign danger probability to men disproportionate to actual risk, amplified by somatic misinterpretation.

Members of communities with high rates of male-perpetrated gender-based violence may develop androphobia through chronic exposure to threat narratives and community trauma, even without direct personal victimisation.

riskFactors.description: Sexual assault, physical abuse, domestic violence, or childhood maltreatment by male perpetrators is the strongest individual risk factor, identified in a large proportion of clinical cases.

riskFactors.factor: Female sex and gender minority identity
riskFactors.description: Androphobia is more commonly reported by women and by gender minority individuals, reflecting both the greater epidemiological exposure to male-perpetrated violence and potential heightened vulnerability due to social position.

PTSD following male-perpetrated trauma substantially raises the probability of developing androphobia as a comorbid condition; PTSD hypervigilance and avoidance can become specific to men as a stimulus class over time.

riskFactors.factor: Pre-existing anxiety disorder or high trait anxiety
riskFactors.description: A background of generalised anxiety disorder, panic disorder, or high trait anxiety lowers the threshold for specific phobia development after any adverse conditioning experience.

riskFactors.factor: Family history of anxiety or phobia
riskFactors.description: A first-degree family history of anxiety disorders increases specific phobia risk approximately twofold; heritability of specific phobia is estimated at 30-40% in twin studies.

riskFactors.factor: Lack of safe social support following trauma
riskFactors.description: Trauma survivors without access to safe, supportive social networks after victimisation are less able to engage in natural recovery processes; chronic isolation can entrench avoidance into a clinical phobia.

riskFactors.factor: Cultural or social norms that normalise male violence
riskFactors.description: Living in communities where male-perpetrated violence carries low social cost and poor institutional response increases the probability that fear conditioning will be maintained and reinforced rather than extinguished over time.

affectedDemographics: Women with a history of male-perpetrated sexual assault, domestic violence, or childhood abuse
Gender minority individuals who have experienced gender-based violence
Survivors of war, forced displacement, or political violence involving male perpetrators
People with comorbid PTSD, particularly complex PTSD arising from prolonged interpersonal trauma
Young women and adolescents in the aftermath of assault or harassment
Individuals in occupations with high exposure to male-perpetrated violence (social work, healthcare in secure settings, law enforcement)
diagnosisOverview: Androphobia is a clinical diagnosis based on structured psychiatric interview and the systematic application of ICD-10 F40.290 or DSM-5 specific phobia criteria, with particular attention to the differential diagnosis with PTSD, social anxiety disorder, and paranoid schizophrenia. A clinician establishes that fear is reliably and specifically triggered by men, is disproportionate to actual risk in the current context, has persisted for at least six months, and produces significant distress or functional impairment. The clinician must distinguish androphobia from PTSD avoidance — in PTSD, avoidance is typically linked to specific trauma reminders, intrusion symptoms, hyperarousal, and negative cognitions that extend across multiple life domains rather than being confined to a fear of men as a stimulus class. The two can co-exist, and both diagnoses may be warranted. Social anxiety disorder differs in that the fear involves negative social evaluation from all people rather than specific fear of men. Assessment tools for trauma history (Trauma Symptom Checklist, PCL-5) and standardised phobia and anxiety measures (Fear Survey Schedule, GAD-7) are used in conjunction. A thorough trauma history taken by a trained clinician is essential but must be conducted with sensitivity to avoid re-traumatisation — the clinician does not require full traumatic recall to make the diagnosis.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Applies DSM-5 or ICD-10 specific phobia criteria systematically and differentiates androphobia from PTSD, social anxiety disorder, and generalised anxiety disorder.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A 20-45 minute clinician-administered interview covering fear onset, intensity, triggers, duration, avoidance, and functional impact; conducted in a safe environment with a trauma-informed clinician.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Screens for and quantifies PTSD symptoms to determine whether PTSD is present alongside androphobia and requires parallel treatment.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A 20-item self-report questionnaire rating PTSD symptom severity over the past month; takes approximately 5-10 minutes.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Quantifies phobic fear intensity across a wide range of stimuli, confirming that elevated fear is specific to men rather than generalised.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A 76-item self-report questionnaire taking 10-15 minutes; places androphobia severity in context of other fears.

diagnosticTests.test: Social Interaction Anxiety Scale (SIAS) and Social Phobia Scale (SPS)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Distinguishes androphobia from social anxiety disorder by identifying whether fear is specific to men or is more broadly related to negative social evaluation across all social interactions.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Two validated questionnaires taking a total of approximately 10 minutes; combined with clinical interview findings.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Screens for trauma-related symptoms including dissociation, anxiety, depression, and sexual disturbance that may complicate androphobia assessment and treatment planning.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A 40-item self-report questionnaire taking approximately 10 minutes; helps quantify trauma symptom burden.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Screen for comorbid generalised anxiety disorder and major depression, which are frequently present alongside androphobia and affect treatment planning.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Two brief validated questionnaires taking under 5 minutes total; widely used in primary and secondary mental health care.

treatmentOverview: Androphobia requires a trauma-informed approach that may differ significantly from the management of non-trauma-related specific phobias. When PTSD is present, best practice guidelines (NICE, WHO, ISTSS) recommend treating PTSD first or concurrently, as unaddressed PTSD maintains the fear-conditioning and hyperarousal that drive androphobia. Trauma-focused CBT (TF-CBT) and EMDR are the recommended PTSD treatments, both of which include processing of the traumatic conditioning experiences that underlie androphobic fear. Once PTSD is stabilised — or where androphobia exists without PTSD — graded in vivo exposure within a CBT framework becomes the first-line treatment for the specific phobia component. The therapeutic relationship is established carefully before any exposure to feared male stimuli is attempted. The pace of the fear hierarchy is individually tailored to the person's trauma history, beginning with images or representations of men before progressing through photographs, familiar trusted men, and ultimately unfamiliar men in public settings. Virtual reality exposure therapy offers a controlled and repeatable environment for the earliest steps of this hierarchy, allowing systematic desensitisation without requiring real-world male presence in the room. Pharmacologically, SSRIs (sertraline, paroxetine) are first-line for comorbid PTSD and major depression, reducing baseline anxiety and enabling engagement with exposure. Prazosin is sometimes added for PTSD-related nightmares and daytime hyperarousal. Dialectical behaviour therapy (DBT) skills training may be integrated when emotional dysregulation from complex PTSD complicates exposure engagement and requires additional stabilisation.

medicalTreatments.name: Trauma-focused cognitive-behavioural therapy (TF-CBT)
medicalTreatments.description: Integrates processing of the traumatic events that conditioned androphobia with graded exposure to men in safe contexts; the trauma-processing component is typically completed before intensive phobia exposure begins.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: TF-CBT achieves PTSD remission in 60-80% of completers; phobia-specific benefits follow as trauma processing reduces the fear-conditioning that maintains androphobia.

medicalTreatments.name: Eye movement desensitisation and reprocessing (EMDR)
medicalTreatments.description: Processes traumatic memories underlying androphobia through bilateral sensory stimulation; NICE and ISTSS recommended for PTSD; an evidence-based alternative to TF-CBT when the phobia is clearly tied to discrete traumatic events.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Achieves PTSD remission in approximately 60-75% of completers in randomised controlled trials; endorsed by WHO and NICE for trauma-related disorders.

medicalTreatments.name: CBT with graded in vivo exposure
medicalTreatments.description: After PTSD stabilisation, systematic approach to a hierarchically arranged series of male-exposure situations — from photographs to familiar male acquaintances to unfamiliar men in public — until fear extinguishes through repeated disconfirmation.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Response rates of 80-90% for specific phobia exposure when applied after PTSD treatment; effect sizes are large in controlled phobia trials.

medicalTreatments.name: SSRI pharmacotherapy (sertraline, paroxetine, fluoxetine)
medicalTreatments.description: First-line medication for comorbid PTSD and major depression; FDA-approved agents (sertraline, paroxetine) reduce intrusion, hyperarousal, and avoidance symptoms, lowering the fear baseline and enabling engagement with exposure therapy.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Significant PTSD symptom reduction in 50-60% of patients; response rates for comorbid depression similar; full response often requires 8-12 weeks at therapeutic dose.

medicalTreatments.name: Prazosin
medicalTreatments.description: An alpha-1 adrenergic blocker used to reduce PTSD-related nightmares and hyperarousal; sometimes combined with SSRIs to address the somatic hypervigilance that amplifies androphobic fear responses.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Meta-analyses show significant reduction in trauma-related nightmares in approximately 60-65% of patients; effect on daytime hyperarousal is more modest.

alternativeTreatments.name: Acceptance and commitment therapy (ACT)
alternativeTreatments.description: Promotes psychological flexibility and acceptance of anxiety in the presence of men as a precondition for valued-life activity; used as a complement or alternative to CBT where avoidance is deeply entrenched.

alternativeTreatments.name: Somatic experiencing and body-based therapies
alternativeTreatments.description: Targets the stored physiological trauma responses — muscle bracing, hyperarousal, dissociation — that maintain androphobia; used by trauma-specialist practitioners alongside CBT and EMDR.

alternativeTreatments.name: Group therapy for trauma survivors
alternativeTreatments.description: Provides peer validation, reduces isolation, and exposes participants to others' recovery narratives; can be a therapeutic precursor to individual exposure-focused work when trust in therapy is low.

whySeeSpecialist: A psychiatrist and/or clinical psychologist with specific expertise in both trauma and anxiety disorders should be involved when androphobia co-occurs with PTSD, complex PTSD, or major depression requiring medication management; when functional impairment is severe (inability to work, seek healthcare, or manage daily life); when previous therapy has not produced adequate change; or when the presentation is complex — dissociation, emotional dysregulation, substance misuse — that requires careful sequencing of treatments.
doctorSelectionGuide: Look for a clinical psychologist or accredited therapist who lists trauma-informed care alongside CBT and who has experience with PTSD, specific phobia, and survivor-centred work. Ask explicitly whether they use graded exposure and whether they pace it according to trauma history. For medication management of comorbid PTSD or depression, a psychiatrist experienced in both anxiety disorders and trauma is preferable. When choosing a therapist, it is clinically appropriate to request a female therapist if the patient's androphobia extends to therapy settings with male practitioners.
preventionStrategies: Access to prompt, trauma-informed support following interpersonal violence reduces the probability that conditioned fear will consolidate into a clinical phobia.
Universal training of healthcare, education, and social care workers in trauma-informed practice ensures that survivors are not re-traumatised in systems meant to support them.
Community-level reduction of male-perpetrated gender-based violence addresses a primary upstream cause of androphobia at a population level.
Early psychological first aid and screening for phobic avoidance after interpersonal trauma can identify androphobia before it becomes severely entrenched.
Ensuring that survivors can access exclusively female clinical teams when needed reduces avoidance of healthcare settings where untreated androphobia would otherwise prevent engagement.
lifestyleModifications: Build a small circle of safe male contacts — friends, family members, or colleagues — as a foundation from which the exposure hierarchy can begin with trusted individuals.
Engage with peer support groups composed of trauma survivors to reduce isolation and build the therapeutic alliance needed for exposure work.
Practice mindfulness and body-awareness exercises to recognise and tolerate physiological anxiety signals without immediately acting on the escape urge.
Communicate androphobia clearly to healthcare providers at registration so that same-sex appointment preferences can be accommodated, reducing avoidance of necessary care.
Use crisis safety planning with a trusted clinician to identify safe spaces, support persons, and coping strategies for unavoidable exposures to men in daily life.

recommended: Regular balanced meals and consistent hydration to maintain stable physiological baseline and reduce somatic anxiety symptoms
Omega-3 fatty acid-rich foods (oily fish, walnuts, flaxseed), which have a small evidence base for reducing anxiety symptoms
Magnesium-containing foods (leafy greens, nuts, seeds) to support parasympathetic nervous system function
avoid: Excess caffeine, which raises cortisol and heart rate, increasing hyperarousal and exacerbating phobic reactivity
Alcohol as a coping mechanism — while providing short-term relief, alcohol impairs extinction learning, worsens depression, and maintains avoidance
Skipping meals or chronic under-eating, which in trauma survivors can destabilise mood and increase vulnerability to fear responses

exerciseGuidelines: Regular moderate aerobic exercise — 150 minutes per week — reduces cortisol, lowers baseline anxiety, and improves mood in PTSD and anxiety disorders. Physical activity in safe, women-only, or known-safe environments is preferable during early recovery. Activities that improve body awareness and self-agency — yoga, martial arts, dance — can be particularly supportive for trauma survivors, as they rebuild confidence in the body's capacity to protect itself. The treating clinician should confirm that exercise activities are compatible with any current trauma processing work.
dailyManagement: Use a daily safety check-in with a trusted support person to maintain social connection without overwhelming avoidance.
Track anxiety levels and avoidance decisions in a diary to identify gradual improvement and discuss with your therapist.
Practise grounding techniques — 5-4-3-2-1 sensory grounding or diaphragmatic breathing — when androphobic fear arises in unavoidable daily situations.
Communicate openly with employers about reasonable workplace accommodations, such as female-only meeting attendance or adjusted workstation placement, during treatment.
Maintain regular therapy appointments and complete between-session exposure tasks at the pace agreed with your therapist.
prognosis: With appropriate trauma-informed CBT and/or EMDR, the prognosis for androphobia is good. When comorbid PTSD is identified and treated first or concurrently, phobia-specific gains follow substantially; specific phobia exposure achieves response rates of 80-90% in completers, and trauma-focused therapies produce PTSD remission in 60-80%. Without treatment, the natural course is chronic impairment, progressive social restriction, and high rates of secondary depression and social isolation. Complex PTSD and severe childhood trauma history are associated with a greater number of sessions required and somewhat lower remission rates, but meaningful quality-of-life improvement is achievable with appropriate care duration, specialist expertise, and attention to pacing. Prognosis worsens with ongoing unsafe living situations or continued contact with perpetrators, making safety planning a prerequisite for effective treatment.
recoveryTimeline: Trauma processing (EMDR or TF-CBT) typically requires 8-16 sessions for defined-trauma PTSD and longer for complex PTSD. Subsequent phobia-specific exposure work adds 4-12 sessions depending on severity. Overall, a person with androphobia and comorbid PTSD might expect meaningful functional improvement at three to six months into a combined treatment programme, with continued gains over the following year. Setbacks after significant life events are common; they do not indicate failure and respond quickly to booster sessions.
complications: Secondary major depressive disorder — chronic isolation, occupational impairment, and the burden of managing severe avoidance substantially elevate depression risk in people with androphobia.
Occupational disability — inability to work in any mixed-gender environment effectively prevents participation in a large portion of the labour market, with financial and social consequences.

Avoidance of healthcare — fear of male medical practitioners causes under-engagement with primary, emergency, and specialist care, delaying diagnosis and treatment of comorbid physical health conditions.
Social isolation and relationship difficulties — avoidance of all men restricts intimate partnerships, family relationships (male relatives), and social networks, compounding psychological distress.
Substance misuse — alcohol and other substances are used by some survivors to manage androphobic fear and PTSD symptoms, creating a secondary substance use disorder that complicates treatment.

supportResources.name: RAINN — Rape, Abuse and Incest National Network
supportResources.description: U.S. resource for sexual assault survivors with crisis support, treatment finder, and information on trauma-related mental health.

supportResources.name: NHS — Post-traumatic stress disorder (PTSD)
supportResources.description: Plain-English UK guidance on PTSD — a common comorbidity with androphobia — including treatments available on the NHS.

supportResources.name: NIMH — Anxiety Disorders and Trauma
supportResources.description: U.S. National Institute of Mental Health overview of anxiety disorders and trauma-related conditions with links to treatment resources.

sources.title: NICE Guideline NG116 — Post-traumatic stress disorder. NICE, 2018
Craske MG et al. Specific phobia. Lancet, 2009;373(9671):1476-1486
Bisson JI et al. Psychological therapies for chronic post-traumatic stress disorder (PTSD) in adults. Cochrane Database of Systematic Reviews, 2013
Wardenaar KJ et al. The cross-national epidemiology of specific phobia in the World Mental Health Surveys. Psychological Medicine, 2017;47(10):1744-1760
WHO — Mental Health Gap Action Programme (mhGAP) Intervention Guide, Version 2.0. World Health Organization, 2016

NIMH — Post-Traumatic Stress Disorder. National Institute of Mental Health, 2023

confusedWithConditions.name: Post-traumatic stress disorder
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: PTSD produces pervasive hyperarousal, intrusive memories, and avoidance across multiple domains triggered by any trauma reminder; androphobia is a discrete stimulus-specific fear of men that can occur with or without PTSD.

confusedWithConditions.name: Social phobia (generalised)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Social phobia involves fear of negative social evaluation from people in general; androphobia is specifically and selectively triggered by men, not by social situations broadly.

confusedWithConditions.name: Agoraphobia
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Agoraphobia is fear of situations where escape is difficult, often with crowded public spaces; it is not stimulus-specific to men and its avoidance profile differs fundamentally from androphobia.

relatedConditions.name: Gynephobia
relatedConditions.relevance: The parallel condition — fear of women — sharing the same ICD-10 subtype (F40.29x), aetiological pathways, and CBT-based treatment approach as androphobia.

relatedConditions.name: Post-traumatic stress disorder
relatedConditions.relevance: PTSD is the most common comorbidity in androphobia following interpersonal trauma; it frequently requires treatment before phobia-specific exposure can begin.

relatedConditions.name: Social phobia (generalised)
relatedConditions.relevance: Social anxiety is frequently comorbid with androphobia and must be differentiated by assessing whether fear is gender-specific or broadly social.

relatedConditions.name: Adjustment disorder with anxiety
relatedConditions.relevance: Acute androphobic reactions following recent traumatic events may initially present as adjustment disorder before becoming sufficiently persistent and impairing to warrant a specific phobia diagnosis.

faqs.question: What is androphobia?
faqs.answer: Androphobia is an intense, persistent, and disabling fear of men. It is classified as an other specified specific phobia (ICD-10: F40.290) and is diagnosed when the fear is disproportionate to actual risk, persists for at least 6 months, and significantly impairs daily life, social functioning, or access to work or healthcare.

faqs.question: What causes androphobia?
faqs.answer: The most commonly identified cause is direct traumatic conditioning from male-perpetrated interpersonal violence — sexual assault, domestic abuse, or childhood maltreatment. Androphobia can also arise through observational learning, cultural trauma transmission, or gradual generalisation of fear from a specific threatening individual to men broadly.

faqs.question: Is androphobia the same as PTSD?
faqs.answer: No, though they frequently co-occur. PTSD produces pervasive hyperarousal, intrusive memories, and avoidance across multiple domains triggered by any reminder of trauma. Androphobia is a discrete stimulus-specific fear of men. Both diagnoses can be present simultaneously, and the treating clinician will assess and address each.

faqs.question: Can androphobia be treated?
faqs.answer: Yes. When PTSD is present, trauma-focused CBT or EMDR is recommended first; phobia-specific gains follow. For androphobia without PTSD, graded in vivo exposure within a CBT framework achieves response rates of 80-90%. SSRIs help when comorbid depression or panic disorder is present.

faqs.question: Is androphobia the same as hating men?
faqs.answer: No. Androphobia is an anxiety disorder — a conditioned fear response — not a philosophical or cultural stance. People with androphobia do not choose to fear men; the fear is automatic, distressing, and unwanted. It produces avoidance and anxiety rather than hostility.

faqs.question: Who gets androphobia?
faqs.answer: Androphobia is reported more frequently in women and gender minority individuals, particularly those with a history of male-perpetrated interpersonal violence. It can affect any person regardless of gender. Survivors of sexual assault, domestic abuse, or childhood maltreatment are at elevated risk.

faqs.question: How is androphobia different from social anxiety?
faqs.answer: Social anxiety disorder involves fear of negative evaluation from people in general — any social interaction carries risk. Androphobia is specifically triggered by men and produces little or no anxiety in equivalent situations involving women. The distinction has direct treatment implications for how exposure hierarchies are constructed.

faqs.question: How is androphobia diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis is made by a trained clinician using structured interview and ICD-10 or DSM-5 criteria, confirming that fear is reliably triggered by men, disproportionate to actual risk, present for at least 6 months, and causing significant distress or impairment. PTSD, social anxiety disorder, and other differentials are formally excluded.

faqs.question: What does exposure therapy involve for androphobia?
faqs.answer: A therapist and patient construct a hierarchy of situations involving men, from least threatening (photographs of men) to most difficult (being near unfamiliar men in public). The patient systematically approaches each step, staying until anxiety subsides. For trauma-related androphobia, this follows PTSD processing work.

faqs.question: Can I request a female doctor or therapist?
faqs.answer: Yes, and clinicians treating androphobia should support this. Requesting female practitioners is a clinically appropriate accommodation during treatment, not a barrier to care. As therapy progresses and the phobia diminishes, the patient may gradually tolerate mixed-gender healthcare settings.

faqs.question: Does androphobia affect work?
faqs.answer: Significantly. Androphobia can make it impossible to work in mixed-gender environments, limiting career choices and income. Reasonable workplace adjustments — female-only meeting attendance, adjusted work locations — can enable employment during treatment. Occupational health referral is valuable in this context.

faqs.question: What medications are used for androphobia?
faqs.answer: SSRIs (sertraline, paroxetine, fluoxetine) are first-line for comorbid PTSD and depression, reducing baseline fear and enabling engagement with exposure therapy. Prazosin helps PTSD-related nightmares and hyperarousal. There is no medication that directly eliminates a specific phobia without accompanying psychological treatment.

faqs.question: Can androphobia affect healthcare access?
faqs.answer: Yes, severely. Fear of male practitioners can lead to avoidance of medical, dental, and emergency care. This can delay diagnosis of serious physical conditions. Informing healthcare providers of androphobia allows appointment accommodation — female practitioners, female chaperones — to be arranged before avoidance prevents care entirely.

faqs.question: Does EMDR work for androphobia?
faqs.answer: EMDR is recommended by NICE and WHO for trauma-related PTSD and is an evidence-based treatment when androphobia arises from discrete traumatic events. By processing the traumatic memories that conditioned the fear, EMDR reduces the intensity of the androphobic response. It is typically followed by or combined with phobia-specific exposure.

faqs.question: Is androphobia a permanent condition?
faqs.answer: Not with treatment. Trauma-focused therapies and phobia-specific CBT produce significant and lasting improvement in the majority of patients. Without treatment, the condition tends to be chronic and to worsen through avoidance reinforcement, but response to therapy remains good across all age groups.

faqs.question: What is the difference between androphobia and gynephobia?
faqs.answer: Androphobia is fear of men; gynephobia is fear of women. Both are specific phobias coded under ICD-10 F40.29x, share similar aetiological pathways, and are treated with the same CBT exposure approach. Either can occur in any gender and does not reflect the patient's sexual orientation.

faqs.question: How long does treatment for androphobia take?
faqs.answer: For androphobia with comorbid PTSD, trauma processing requires 8-16 sessions; phobia-specific exposure adds 4-12 sessions. Most people with combined treatment can see meaningful functional improvement within 3-6 months. Complex PTSD requires a longer and more individualised treatment timeline.

faqs.question: Can androphobia cause depression?
faqs.answer: Yes. Chronic isolation, inability to work or socialise freely, and the burden of managing severe avoidance all substantially increase the risk of comorbid major depressive disorder. Depression, when present, requires treatment alongside the phobia and may benefit from SSRI pharmacotherapy.

faqs.question: What self-help strategies help with androphobia?
faqs.answer: Building a small circle of trusted safe men as a foundation for gradual exposure, practising grounding and breathing techniques, maintaining peer support connections, and keeping a daily record of successful small exposures all support recovery alongside formal therapy.

faqs.question: Can children or adolescents develop androphobia?
faqs.answer: Yes. Androphobia that arises following childhood maltreatment or sexual abuse can present in adolescence with school refusal, avoidance of male teachers or peers, and distress during routine mixed-gender activities. Child and adolescent mental health services with trauma expertise should be involved when androphobia is identified in young people.

metaDescription: Androphobia is an intense fear of men that causes panic and avoidance, often linked to trauma. Learn symptoms, trauma-informed CBT treatment, and diagnosis.
